title: Caring for the Geriatric Patient
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine

items.subtopic: Caring for the Geriatric Patient | DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 1)
items.title: Demographic Shifts and Healthcare Implications
items.content: The aging population is rapidly increasing, with those over 80 being the fastest growing segment. This demographic shift will significantly impact healthcare resource consumption in high-income countries.
keyPoints: Rapid increase in older adults seeking health care.
1 in 6 people worldwide will be 65 or older by 2050.
Close to half of those over 80 have three chronic conditions; about one-third have four or more.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · Caring for the Geriatric Patient | DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Caring for the Geriatric Patient | DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 1)
items.title: Principles of Effective Geriatric Care
items.content: Effective geriatric care requires a comprehensive, multidisciplinary approach that focuses on functional ability and quality of life rather than cure.
keyPoints: Aging is not a disease; it increases susceptibility to diseases due to diminished physiologic reserve.
Multimorbidity and multifactorial origins necessitate a comprehensive evaluation and management approach.
Focus on functional ability and quality of life as key goals of care.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · Caring for the Geriatric Patient | DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: Caring for the Geriatric Patient | DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 1)
items.title: The '5M's Framework in Geriatrics
items.content: The '5M’s' framework—mentation, medication, mobility, multicomplexity, and matters most—guides person-centered care for older adults.
keyPoints: Mentation: Attention to cognitive disorders like mild cognitive impairment and dementia is crucial.
Medication: Polypharmacy and potentially inappropriate medications are common; careful reconciliation is essential.
Mobility: Critical for function, quality of life, and fall risk.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · Caring for the Geriatric Patient | DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 2) | PERSON-CENTERED CARE
items.title: Why Person-Centered Care is Essential in Geriatric Medicine
items.content: Person-centered care prioritizes individualized, goal-oriented plans that consider the unique medical, functional, and psychosocial needs of older adults. This approach is vital due to the complexity of managing multimorbidity in this population.
keyPoints: Care depends on evidence-based medicine and individual preferences.
Key elements include: (1) individualized care plans; (2) ongoing review of goals; (3) integrated communication; (4) education for providers and patients; (5) performance measurement with feedback.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 2) | PERSON-CENTERED CARE

items.subtopic: DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 2) | PERSON-CENTERED CARE
items.title: How to Implement Person-Centered Care in Geriatric Practice
items.content: Implementing person-centered care involves using tools like prognosis estimation and 'Choosing Wisely' recommendations. These resources help tailor medical decisions to the specific needs and preferences of older adults.
keyPoints: Tools include ePrognosis and AGS/AMDA's 'Choosing Wisely'.
Focus on comprehensive approaches addressing medical conditions, function, quality of life, comfort, and dignity.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · DEMOGRAPHICS OF AGING AND ITS IMPLICATIONS FOR CARE OF OLDER ADULTS (part 2) | PERSON-CENTERED CARE

items.title: Importance of Screening for Functional Impairments in Geriatric Patients
items.content: Screening questions as part of a geriatric review of systems are crucial due to the high prevalence and impact of functional impairments, disabilities, cognitive disorders, and undetected geriatric conditions. Positive responses should lead to further assessments using validated tools.
keyPoints: Functional impairments and disabilities are prevalent in older adults.
Screening questions can identify issues that may cause patient safety concerns.
Further assessment is prompted by positive screening responses.
Standardized tools include activities of daily living scales, depression scales, sleep questionnaires, and mental status examinations.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COMPREHENSIVE GERIATRIC ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Evaluating Medical Decision-Making Capacity in Older Adults
items.content: Assessing decision-making capacity is essential to protect older adults from abuse and preserve autonomy. It involves ensuring patients understand information, have no major sensory impairments, and are not under undue influence.
keyPoints: Decision-making capacity assessment is crucial for patient autonomy.
Aging increases the risk of cognitive impairment affecting decision-making.
Capacity should be differentiated from legal incompetence.
Tools like MacCAT-T and CCTI help assess capacity in specific contexts.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COMPREHENSIVE GERIATRIC ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Assessment of Older Drivers for Safety
items.content: Routine assessment of older drivers is necessary due to their increased risk of fatal crashes, especially those aged ≥85 years. Evaluations should consider sensory, functional, and cognitive impairments, as well as the impact of certain medications.
keyPoints: Older adults face a higher risk of fatal crashes, with a ninefold increase for those ≥85 years old.
Driving cessation can lead to social isolation and depression.
Assess sensory, functional, and cognitive impairments impacting driving safety.
Medications like antianxiety agents, narcotic analgesics, antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, and drugs with strong anticholinergic properties can impair driving.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COMPREHENSIVE GERIATRIC ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Driving Impairment in Geriatric Patients
items.content: Suspected driving impairment in older adults involves balancing independence, safety, and liability. Physicians may be required to report unsafe drivers in some states. Evaluation should focus on reversible causes like vision or hearing impairments. No single test predicts unsafe driving; a combination of neuropsychological and on-road testing is recommended.
keyPoints: Driving impairment decisions involve patient independence, family concerns, and physician liability.
Physicians may need to report unsafe older drivers in some states.
Evaluation should target reversible causes like vision or hearing impairments.
No single test predicts unsafe driving; use neuropsychological testing and on-road testing.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COMPREHENSIVE GERIATRIC ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.title: Choosing Wisely Recommendations for Geriatric Care
items.content: The AGS provides recommendations to avoid unnecessary interventions in geriatric patients. These include not recommending percutaneous feeding tubes for advanced dementia, avoiding antipsychotics as first-line treatment for dementia symptoms, and using metformin preferentially for glycemic control.
keyPoints: Avoid percutaneous feeding tubes in advanced dementia; offer oral assisted feeding instead.
Do not use antipsychotics as the first choice for behavioral symptoms of dementia.
Use metformin to achieve hemoglobin A1c <7.5% in most older adults.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COMPREHENSIVE GERIATRIC ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.title: Interpreting Diagnostic Tests in Older Adults
items.content: Physiologic changes with aging affect diagnostic test results, making interpretation challenging. Ambulatory cardiac monitoring may identify arrhythmias that need symptom correlation before treatment. Advanced imaging can reveal incidental findings with a ~20% malignancy rate. Abnormal tests like low hemoglobin or abnormal thyroid function require further evaluation.
keyPoints: Aging affects diagnostic test results; interpret cautiously.
Ambulatory cardiac monitoring may identify arrhythmias needing symptom correlation before treatment.
Advanced imaging can show incidental findings with ~20% malignancy rate.
Abnormal tests (e.g., low hemoglobin, abnormal thyroid function) require further evaluation.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COMPREHENSIVE GERIATRIC ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.title: Age-Appropriate Screening: Balancing Benefits and Risks
items.content: Screening tests in geriatric patients should be tailored to the individual's life expectancy, with a focus on preventing morbidity from diseases that could impact their quality of life. The decision to screen must weigh the potential benefits against risks and costs, especially considering limited life expectancy.
keyPoints: U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends colorectal cancer screening up to age 75; for ages 76-85, consider colonoscopy only if never screened before and healthy enough for treatment if needed.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · PREVENTION IN OLDER ADULTS

items.title: Vaccination Strategies in Older Adults: Prioritizing Common Infections
items.content: Vaccinations are crucial in older adults to prevent infections that could lead to serious complications. Routine vaccinations against influenza, pneumococcus, and shingles are recommended due to their prevalence in this age group. Vaccination recommendations for COVID-19 and other respiratory viruses should be regularly updated.
keyPoints: CDC recommends routine vaccination against influenza, pneumococcus, and shingles for older adults.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · PREVENTION IN OLDER ADULTS

items.title: Addressing STDs in Older Adults: Awareness and Screening
items.content: Older adults remain sexually active, with a notable portion of those over 80 engaging in sexual activity. Despite lower incidence rates, screening for STDs is important when symptoms are present, especially given the potential lack of awareness about safe practices.
keyPoints: Up to a quarter of individuals in their 80s consider themselves sexually active; older adults may have a lower awareness of safe sexual practices.
In the U.S., approximately 450 per 100,000 persons above age 55 are infected with HIV.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · PREVENTION IN OLDER ADULTS

items.title: HIV Management in Older Adults: Increasing Prevalence and Life Expectancy
items.content: The prevalence of HIV is rising among older adults due to increased life expectancy from antiretroviral therapy and new infections. This necessitates awareness and management strategies tailored for this demographic.
keyPoints: 450 per 100,000 persons above age 55 are infected with HIV in the U.S.; highly active antiretroviral therapy has increased life expectancy for patients with HIV.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · PREVENTION IN OLDER ADULTS

items.title: Blood Pressure Targets in Geriatric Hypertension Management
items.content: The management of hypertension in older adults involves balancing cardiovascular benefits against treatment-related risks. Blood pressure targets vary based on guidelines and individual patient factors such as comorbidities and risk of falls.
keyPoints: 70% of older adults in the U.S. have hypertension.
European Society of Cardiology/European Society of Hypertension recommends pharmacologic treatment for SBP ≥160 mmHg in those 80 years or older.
American College of Physicians suggests starting treatment if SBP is ≥150 mmHg.
HYVET study showed benefits of targeting SBP ≤150 mmHg in reducing stroke, heart failure, and mortality risk in patients ≥80 years old.
SPRINT trial indicated intensive SBP reduction (<140 vs. 120 mmHg) reduced cardiovascular events but excluded high-risk groups like those with diabetes or history of stroke.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · HYPERTENSION

items.title: Individualized Hypertension Management in Older Adults
items.content: A person-centered approach is crucial for managing hypertension in older adults, considering factors such as comorbidities and risk of adverse effects like postural hypotension.
keyPoints: For older patients with minimal comorbidity and low fall risk, SBP targets <130 mmHg are beneficial.
Higher SBP targets (e.g., <150 mmHg) are safer for those with diabetes, heart failure, or postural hypotension.
Aggressive BP management may benefit patients with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation or coronary artery disease.
SPRINT trial exclusions highlight the increased risk of adverse effects in aggressive treatment among high-risk groups.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · HYPERTENSION

items.title: Hypoglycemia Risk in Older Adults with Diabetes
items.content: Older diabetic patients, particularly those aged 75 or older, have a significantly increased risk of hypoglycemia, which can lead to emergency department visits and is associated with progressive cognitive decline. This risk necessitates careful management strategies.
keyPoints: Hypoglycemic episodes are linked to cognitive decline in older adults.
Patients aged 75 or older have twice the risk of visiting the emergency department due to hypoglycemia.
Intensive glycemic control does not reduce major macrovascular events in older adults.
AGS and ADA guidelines recommend an HbA1c target of <7.5% for most older adults, considering potential harms outweigh benefits.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · DIABETES

items.title: Tailored Diabetes Management in Geriatric Patients
items.content: Diabetes management in the geriatric population should be individualized based on functional status, comorbidities, and life expectancy. Avoiding insulin when possible is advised due to hypoglycemia risks, with continuous glucose monitoring as a potential aid for those requiring insulin.
keyPoints: Treatment goals should consider patient's functional status, coexisting conditions, social support, and personal goals.
Insulin use should be minimized; avoid long-term sliding-scale insulin due to high hypoglycemia risk.
Continuous glucose monitoring may benefit those on insulin.
Regular examinations for neuropathy and retinopathy are crucial in older diabetic patients.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · DIABETES

items.title: When to Use Statins for Primary Prevention in Older Adults
items.content: The use of statins for primary prevention in older adults, particularly those over 75 years old, is controversial. While there are benefits demonstrated in secondary analyses for selected subgroups, life expectancy and increased risk of muscle-related side effects with coexisting risk factors limit their utility.
keyPoints: Benefits of statins for primary prevention in those ≥75 years old are debated.
Secondary prevention benefits are well-supported.
Life expectancy is a limiting factor for meaningful outcomes.
Risk of myositis and rhabdomyolysis increases with other risk factors.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · HYPERLIPIDEMIA

items.title: Discontinuation of Statins in Older Adults at End-of-Life
items.content: For older adults with a life expectancy of up to 1 year, discontinuing statins is associated with improved quality of life and reduced polypharmacy burden without increased cardiovascular risk.
keyPoints: A third of older people are maintained on statins at the end of life.
Discontinuation in those with ≤1-year life expectancy improves quality of life.
Reduces polypharmacy burden without increasing cardiovascular complications.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · HYPERLIPIDEMIA

items.title: NSAIDs in Older Patients with OA: Risks and Alternatives
items.content: In older patients, NSAID use for osteoarthritis (OA) is associated with significant risks due to potential adverse effects such as GI bleeding, renal function deterioration, fluid retention, hypertension, and exacerbation of congestive heart failure. Additionally, the risk increases when combined with anticoagulants or platelet aggregation inhibitors.
keyPoints: NSAIDs are linked to GI bleeding, worsening renal function, fluid retention, hypertension, and congestive heart failure exacerbation.
Older patients on anticoagulants or platelet aggregation inhibitors face increased bleeding risk with NSAID use.
Topical NSAIDs and analgesic creams (e.g., 1% diclofenac) are better tolerated alternatives.
Acetaminophen at doses of 1 g 2–3 times daily should be the primary pharmacologic treatment.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · OSTEOARTHRITIS AND CHRONIC PAIN

items.title: Pharmacologic Management: Tramadol and Opioids in Older Patients with OA
items.content: For older patients not responding to acetaminophen, tramadol or short-acting narcotics may be considered. Tramadol has a lower addiction potential but must be used cautiously due to seizure risks and interactions with SSRIs/SNRIs.
keyPoints: Tramadol is an option for those unresponsive to acetaminophen; it has less addiction risk than stronger opioids.
Contraindications include seizure disorders and caution with SSRI/SNRI use to prevent serotonin toxicity.
Opioids can be prescribed for pain relief in older patients, especially post-surgery, but require careful monitoring for abuse.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · OSTEOARTHRITIS AND CHRONIC PAIN

items.title: Nonpharmacologic Interventions and Surgical Options for OA in Older Patients
items.content: Nonpharmacologic interventions such as physical therapy, heat/ice application, and aquatic therapies are effective first-line treatments. Surgical options like total knee replacement can improve function and quality of life in selected older patients.
keyPoints: First-line treatment includes nonpharmacologic interventions supervised by trained professionals.
Options include stretching, strengthening exercises, heat/ice therapy, massage, swimming, bracing, acupuncture, and electrical stimulation.
Total knee replacement is effective for generally healthy older patients and should be considered in higher-risk individuals with proper pre-habilitation.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · OSTEOARTHRITIS AND CHRONIC PAIN

items.title: Importance of Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment in Older Adults with Cancer
items.content: Older adults often experience declines in functional status post-chemotherapy, primarily due to comorbidities and baseline functional status rather than age alone. A comprehensive geriatric assessment is recommended to predict which older adults will tolerate and benefit most from cancer treatment.
keyPoints: More than half of new cancer cases occur after age 65.
Age alone is not a predictor of harm in cancer treatment, but comorbidities are significant factors.
Comprehensive geriatric assessment helps predict tolerance to cancer treatments.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · CANCER | ANEMIA

items.title: Challenges and Considerations in Cancer Treatment for Older Adults
items.content: Treatment decisions for older adults with cancer are complex due to shorter life expectancy and multiple comorbidities. A person-centered approach integrating geriatric and palliative care principles is essential.
keyPoints: Shorter life expectancy and multiple comorbidities complicate treatment decisions in older adults.
A person-centered approach, incorporating geriatric and palliative care principles, is crucial.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · CANCER | ANEMIA

items.title: Approach to Anemia Evaluation in Older Adults
items.content: Low hemoglobin or hematocrit is common but not normal in older adults. A basic evaluation including complete blood count and iron studies should be conducted. Iron deficiency is the most common cause of anemia, while unexplained anemia often presents as normocytic with low reticulocyte counts.
keyPoints: Low hemoglobin or hematocrit is not a normal age-related change in older adults.
Basic evaluation includes complete blood count, peripheral red blood cell smear, reticulocyte count, and iron studies.
Iron deficiency is the most common cause of anemia; unexplained anemia is often normocytic with low reticulocyte counts.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · CANCER | ANEMIA

items.title: Echocardiography in Geriatric Heart Failure Management
items.content: Echocardiography is crucial for determining ejection fraction, which guides the management of heart failure (HF) in older adults. This helps differentiate between HF with preserved and reduced ejection fractions.
keyPoints: 20% of individuals ≥80 years old have a diagnosis of HF
Two-thirds of these cases involve preserved ejection fraction
Echocardiography is essential for management decisions
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · HEART FAILURE

items.title: Treatment Considerations in Frail Older Adults with Heart Failure
items.content: The treatment of heart failure in frail older adults must consider the type of ejection fraction and medication tolerability. Newer therapies like SGLT2 inhibitors have shown efficacy.
keyPoints: Management depends on cardiac ejection fraction (preserved vs reduced)
Medications may be poorly tolerated due to hypotension, electrolyte imbalances, volume depletion
Newer agents such as SGLT2i improve outcomes in specific HF types
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · HEART FAILURE

items.title: Importance of Regular Screening for Falls in Geriatric Patients
items.content: Internists should regularly screen older patients for falls due to the high prevalence and significant consequences. Questions such as 'Have you fallen in the past year?' help identify those at risk.
keyPoints: One in three community-dwelling and one in two LTC facility residents fall annually.
Falls can lead to loss of function, decreased quality of life, and increased mortality.
Regular screening questions: 'Have you fallen in the past year?', 'Are you afraid of falling?', 'Do you have trouble climbing stairs or rising from chairs?'
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · FALLS

items.title: Multifactorial Evaluation and Management of Falls in Older Adults
items.content: Evaluating falls requires identifying multiple risk factors. Many falls are mechanical but may have underlying reversible conditions. Immediate management includes injury assessment and addressing acute illnesses.
keyPoints: Falls often have multifactorial causes, requiring thorough evaluation.
Half of 'unexplained falls' in older people with dementia may be due to near-syncope or syncope from postural hypotension.
Management involves physical therapy, balance training, and possibly Tai Chi; vitamin D replacement is common but high-dose (60,000 IU monthly) increases fall risk.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · FALLS

items.title: Anticoagulation Management in High-Risk Fall Patients with Atrial Fibrillation
items.content: In older adults at high fall risk with atrial fibrillation, the benefits of anticoagulation outweigh bleeding risks. Consideration for a left atrial appendage closure device is advised.
keyPoints: Anticoagulants are preferred despite bleeding risks due to lower stroke risk.
Atrial fibrillation in high fall-risk patients warrants careful anticoagulant management.
Left atrial appendage closure devices may be considered for these patients.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · FALLS

items.title: Understanding Polypharmacy in Geriatrics: Risks and Prevalence
items.content: Polypharmacy, defined as the use of five to nine medications simultaneously, is prevalent among older adults due to multimorbidity and extensive clinical guidelines. It poses significant risks including adverse drug events leading to hospitalizations.
keyPoints: 40% of U.S. population aged 65+ take 5-9 medications; nearly 20% take 10 or more.
Adverse drug events from polypharmacy cause >100,000 hospitalizations annually.
Main culprits include warfarin, antiplatelet agents, insulin, and hypoglycemic agents.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · POLYPHARMACY

items.title: Evaluating Polypharmacy: Importance of Medication Reconciliation
items.content: Medication reconciliation is crucial at each care transition to minimize adverse drug effects, ensure adherence, and evaluate the effectiveness of therapy in older patients.
keyPoints: Internists should perform careful medication reconciliation during office visits and care transitions.
Considerations include unclear diagnosis or indication, uncertain dose/route, stop date, hold parameters, lab tests needed, dosage changes, duplication, restarts, and potential interactions.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · POLYPHARMACY

items.title: Deprescribing in Geriatrics: A Critical Approach to Medication Management
items.content: Deprescribing involves carefully reducing or stopping medications that may no longer be necessary, especially during care transitions. It is essential for minimizing adverse effects and improving patient-centered care.
keyPoints: Focus on deprescribing drugs like diuretics with postural hypotension, benzodiazepines, anticholinergic agents, PPIs with unclear indications, cholinesterase inhibitors in severe cognitive impairment, hypoglycemic agents in multimorbid patients, and statins/prophylactic aspirin near end of life.
AGS's Beers criteria and STOPP are useful for identifying inappropriate medications.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · POLYPHARMACY

items.subtopic: COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT: DELIRIUM AND DEMENTIA | • Circumstances surrounding the fall | Physical Examination
items.title: Identifying Risk Factors for Falls in Geriatric Patients with Cognitive Impairment
items.content: Understanding and identifying risk factors for falls is crucial in managing geriatric patients, especially those with cognitive impairment. Delirium, prevalent in up to 40% of hospitalized older adults, significantly increases fall risks due to its association with increased morbidity and mortality.
keyPoints: Delirium occurs in up to 40% of hospitalized older patients.
Risk factors for falls include changes in posture, medication intake, environmental hazards like loose rugs or slippery floors.
Premonitory symptoms such as dizziness, cardiovascular issues, focal neurologic signs, and recent volume depletion should be assessed.
Medications contributing to fall risk include diuretics, antihypertensives, psychotropics, and hypoglycemic drugs.
Physical examination should exclude physical injury and acute illness, assess vital signs, skin turgor, and check for focal neurologic signs.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT: DELIRIUM AND DEMENTIA | • Circumstances surrounding the fall | Physical Examination

items.subtopic: COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT: DELIRIUM AND DEMENTIA | • Circumstances surrounding the fall | Physical Examination
items.title: Conducting a Thorough Physical Examination to Prevent Falls in Geriatric Patients
items.content: A comprehensive physical examination is essential for preventing falls among geriatric patients, particularly those with cognitive impairment. This includes evaluating for injuries, acute illnesses, and conditions that increase fall risk.
keyPoints: Exclude head trauma, hip range of motion issues, and signs of trauma during the physical exam.
Check for bruising in patients on anticoagulants or platelet inhibitors.
Assess vital signs, including postural changes if safe, and perform a fingerstick glucose test for diabetics.
Evaluate skin turgor for volume depletion and look for signs of acute respiratory, cardiovascular, or abdominal conditions.
Observe gait and balance using the Get Up and Go test to identify abnormalities like short steps, poor foot elevation, or wide-based gait.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT: DELIRIUM AND DEMENTIA | • Circumstances surrounding the fall | Physical Examination

items.title: Guidelines for Laboratory and Imaging in Geriatric Patients
items.content: Laboratory and imaging studies should be guided by the patient's history and physical examination. Common tests include complete blood count, basic metabolic panel, urinalysis (if urinary symptoms are present), electrocardiogram (for suspected acute coronary syndrome or known cardiovascular disease), X-rays for fractures, brain imaging for signs of subdural hematoma or stroke, cardiac monitoring for syncope or near-syncope history, and electroencephalography for seizure history.
keyPoints: Laboratory studies should be guided by clinical presentation.
Urinalysis is indicated if urinary symptoms are present.
Electrocardiogram is used in patients with suspected acute coronary syndrome or significant cardiovascular disease.
Brain imaging is warranted if there are signs of subdural hematoma or stroke.
Cardiac monitoring is recommended for patients with a history suggestive of syncope or near-syncope.
Electroencephalography should be considered in patients with a history suggestive of seizure.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · Laboratory and/or Imaging Studies

items.title: Cognitive Impairment and Dementia Prevalence in Geriatric Patients
items.content: Normal aging does not cause cognitive dysfunction severe enough to be considered dementia. Mild cognitive impairment (MCI) affects just over 20% of people over age 70 in the U.S., with up to 15-20% progressing to dementia within a year. Most individuals with MCI may progress to dementia within five years.
keyPoints: Normal aging does not cause severe cognitive dysfunction indicative of dementia.
Mild cognitive impairment (MCI) affects over 20% of people over age 70 in the U.S.
15-20% of individuals with MCI may progress to dementia within a year.
Most individuals with MCI are likely to progress to dementia within five years.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · Laboratory and/or Imaging Studies

items.subtopic: TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 1)
items.title: Evaluation of Delirium as a Medical Emergency
items.content: Delirium in geriatric patients should be treated as a medical emergency due to its potential manifestation of underlying critical illness. The evaluation involves identifying predisposing and precipitating factors, using the Confusion Assessment Method for diagnosis.
keyPoints: New onset of delirium is a medical emergency.
Predisposing factors include hearing or visual impairment, depression, laboratory abnormalities, uncontrolled pain, infections, exacerbation of chronic illnesses, and substance use history.
Confusion Assessment Method requires acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganized thinking, or altered level of consciousness.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 1)
items.title: Screening and Evaluation for Cognitive Impairment
items.content: Screening older adults for cognitive impairment is controversial, but early interventions can be beneficial. Neuropsychological testing differentiates between MCI and dementia; the Mini-Cog is a sensitive screening tool.
keyPoints: Screening benefits are controversial but early intervention may help.
Neuropsychological testing helps differentiate MCI from dementia.
Mini-Cog includes a three-item recall test and clock drawing.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 1)
items.title: Pharmacologic Management of Delirium
items.content: In managing delirium, avoid or discontinue medications worsening cognitive function. Use low-dose risperidone or quetiapine for severe symptoms; avoid more sedating antipsychotics and benzodiazepines unless necessary.
keyPoints: Avoid medications that worsen cognitive function in delirious patients.
Use low-dose risperidone (0.25–0.5 mg) or quetiapine (12.5–50 mg at night).
Avoid more sedating antipsychotics and benzodiazepines unless necessary.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 2)
items.title: Cholinesterase Inhibitors in Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Cholinesterase inhibitors are used to delay the progression of Alzheimer's disease and extend the time before institutional placement is necessary. However, they come with significant risks including GI side effects like nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, nightmares, bradycardia, syncope, falls, and potential pacemaker placement.
keyPoints: Cholinesterase inhibitors delay progression of Alzheimer's disease
Increase time before institutional placement is needed
GI side effects: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea
Other side effects: nightmares, bradycardia, syncope, falls, pacemaker placement
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 2)
items.title: Emerging Therapies and Biomarkers in Alzheimer's Disease
items.content: Recent advancements include more accurate biomarker testing, such as plasma p-tau 217, which identifies amyloid-beta and predicts cognitive decline. Antiamyloid antibodies can clear amyloid on PET scans but have not shown major impacts on cognition by the end of 2024, with side effects like intracerebral hemorrhage and high costs.
keyPoints: Plasma p-tau 217 identifies amyloid-beta and predicts cognitive decline
Antiamyloid antibodies approved for clearing amyloid on PET scans
No major impacts on cognition by end of 2024
Side effects: intracerebral hemorrhage
High out-of-pocket cost
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · TABLE 489-7 General Recommendations for Geriatric Prescribing (part 2)

items.title: Prevalence and Impact of Urinary Incontinence in Older Adults
items.content: Urinary incontinence affects a significant portion of the older adult population, with one-third of women and 15-20% of men over age 65 experiencing some degree. It is more prevalent in nursing homes (60-80%). Incontinence can lead to social isolation, depression, and increased risk for falls.
keyPoints: Approximately one in three women and 15–20% of men older than age 65 years have urinary incontinence.
Prevalence is as high as 60–80% in many nursing homes.
Incontinence can lead to social isolation, depression, and increased risk for falls.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · URINARY INCONTINENCE AND OVERACTIVE BLADDER

items.title: Characteristics and Risks of Overactive Bladder (OAB)
items.content: Overactive bladder affects about 40% of older adults, characterized by urinary urgency, frequency, and nocturia. Nocturia alone should prompt evaluation for sleep disorders. OAB increases fall risk due to rushing to the toilet.
keyPoints: ~40% of older people suffer from overactive bladder (OAB).
Symptoms include urinary urgency, frequency, and nocturia.
Nocturia as a predominant symptom warrants investigation into sleep disorders.
Increased fall risk associated with OAB due to rushing to the toilet.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · URINARY INCONTINENCE AND OVERACTIVE BLADDER

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Urinary Incontinence in Older Adults
items.content: Internists should inquire about urinary symptoms, as they are often underreported. The 3IQ questionnaire helps differentiate between urge and stress incontinence. History and physical examination focus on identifying reversible causes and specific lower urinary tract symptoms.
keyPoints: Simple questions can help identify incontinent patients.
The 3IQ questionnaire distinguishes between urge and stress incontinence.
History and physical exam should identify potentially reversible causes.

items.content: For older adults with gait disorders or a history of falling, using a urinal, bedside commode, or external catheter is recommended to prevent falls. Ensuring a clear path if they insist on walking to the bathroom is also crucial.

items.title: Prevalence and Impact of Sleep Disorders in Older Adults
items.content: Sleep disorders, including insomnia, OSA, RLS, and PLMS, are prevalent among older adults with significant impacts on health-related quality of life, cognitive function, and healthcare utilization.
keyPoints: Insomnia affects close to 30% of people older than 65.
OSA occurs in ~10% of older adults but is underreported.
RLS prevalence increases in those older than 70, affecting 5–10% of adults and more common in women.
PLMS can be found in up to 45% of older people.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · SLEEP DISORDERS

items.title: Management Strategies for Sleep Disorders in Geriatric Patients
items.content: Nonpharmacologic management is preferred for sleep disorders in the elderly, with specific pharmacological options like melatonin or low-dose trazodone being safer alternatives to benzodiazepines.
keyPoints: Nonpharmacologic interventions should be primary for managing sleep disorders.
Benzodiazepine hypnotics are associated with cognitive impairment and increased fall risk, thus should be avoided when possible.
Melatonin or low-dose trazodone may be used for sleep-onset insomnia as safer alternatives.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · SLEEP DISORDERS

items.title: Understanding Frailty in Geriatric Patients: Definition, Prevalence, and Impact
items.content: Frailty is a syndrome characterized by increased vulnerability due to a decline in physiologic reserve and function across multiple systems. It is crucial for clinicians to recognize frailty as it influences treatment decisions and outcomes. The prevalence of frailty varies based on age and setting, with higher rates observed in older populations.
keyPoints: Frailty is defined by criteria such as Fried's five characteristics: unintentional weight loss, weakness, exhaustion, slowness, and low activity.
Prevalence of frailty in community-dwelling adults aged 65+ averages 10–14%, increasing to nearly 16% for ages 80-84 and 26% for those 85 or older.
In hospitalized or institutionalized older patients, prevalence ranges from about 27% to up to 80%.
Frailty shows a U-shaped relationship with BMI, being higher in individuals with both low and very high BMI.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · FRAILTY

items.title: Pathophysiology and Management of Frailty: A Multidimensional Approach
items.content: Frailty involves cellular changes like mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and stem cell exhaustion leading to physiologic alterations such as inflammation and sarcopenia. Management focuses on physical activity, nutritional supplementation, chronic condition optimization, and medication management to improve frailty components.
keyPoints: Pathophysiology includes cellular changes (e.g., mitochondrial dysfunction), physiological alterations (e.g., inflammation), and phenotypic decline in function.
Management strategies include person-centered physical activity programs and nutritional supplementation to enhance muscle strength and mobility.
Optimizing chronic condition management and reducing polypharmacy can help reverse or slow frailty progression.
Emerging research in geroscience explores potential interventions like metformin and senolytic agents for frailty.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · FRAILTY

items.subtopic: ELDER ABUSE AND NEGLECT | COVID-19 | END-OF-LIFE AND PALLIATIVE CARE
items.title: Elder Abuse and Neglect: Screening and Reporting
items.content: Screening for elder abuse and neglect is crucial due to underreporting and significant consequences. Older adults should be screened without caregiver presence using specific questions about safety, threats, and financial exploitation.
keyPoints: Incidence of elder abuse and neglect over 12 months: at least 8–10%
Screening questions include: 'Do you ever feel unsafe where you live?', 'Has anyone ever threatened or hurt you?', 'Has anyone been taking your money without your permission?'
Report suspected cases to local/state agencies for investigation and patient safety.

items.title: COVID-19 Impact on Older Adults
items.content: The COVID-19 pandemic significantly affected older adults, especially those in nursing homes and assisted living facilities. They are at higher risk for adverse outcomes compared to younger populations.
keyPoints: Older adults have a greater risk of adverse outcomes from COVID-19 infection, particularly with comorbidities
Nursing home staffing was severely impacted during the pandemic
Internists should refer to CDC guidelines on vaccination regularly.

items.title: End-of-Life and Palliative Care Decision-Making in Geriatrics
items.content: Decision-making for palliative care in older adults is complex due to multimorbidity. Internists should initiate discussions on end-of-life care early, especially when life expectancy is limited.
keyPoints: Limited life expectancy is crucial in end-of-life decisions for older patients
CPR survivability in hospitalized patients age 65 and older: <20%
CPR survivability in nursing home residents is almost zero
Enteral feeding tubes are not recommended for patients with end-stage dementia
Advance directives should be encouraged before health crises

items.title: Importance of Innovative Geriatric Care Models in Value-Based Health Systems
items.content: Innovative geriatric care models, such as outpatient comprehensive assessments and home-based programs, are crucial for addressing the complex needs of elderly patients with multimorbidity. These models are increasingly vital in value-based health systems aimed at improving quality and effectiveness.
keyPoints: Models include outpatient comprehensive geriatric assessment programs, inpatient acute care for the elderly (ACE) units, consultation and co-management services, and home-based programs.
These models address multimorbidity, functional and cognitive impairment, and social support challenges.

items.title: Role of Care Transition Interventions in Reducing Readmissions for Geriatric Patients
items.content: Care transition interventions are critical to mitigate complications during discharge from acute medical or psychiatric hospitals, PAC facilities, and home care programs. These interventions aim to reduce communication problems and medical errors that arise due to fragmented care.
keyPoints: Geriatric patients face high vulnerability to complications at discharge points.
Reimbursement changes and penalties for hospital readmissions have spurred the development of these interventions.

items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why polypharmacy increases the risk of adverse drug events.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · POLYPHARMACY

items.assertion: The AGS's Beers criteria list drugs that may be appropriate for older people.
items.reason: The Beers criteria include a comprehensive list of potentially inappropriate medications for older adults.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the Beers criteria list drugs that may be inappropriate, not appropriate.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · POLYPHARMACY

items.subtopic: COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT: DELIRIUM AND DEMENTIA | • Circumstances surrounding the fall | Physical Examination
items.assertion: Podiatric conditions can increase the risk of falls in geriatric patients.
items.reason: Conditions such as calluses, bunions, and poorly fitted shoes are mentioned as factors that contribute to falls.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Caring for the Geriatric Patient · COGNITIVE IMPAIRMENT: DELIRIUM AND DEMENTIA | • Circumstances surrounding the fall | Physical Examination

items.assertion: Most episodes of delirium clear within a few days if the underlying cause is identified and treated.
items.reason: Delirium may persist for weeks or months after an acute hospitalization.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why most episodes of delirium clear quickly.

items.assertion: The Confusion Assessment Method requires an acute onset and fluctuating course.
items.reason: It also requires inattention and disorganized thinking or altered level of consciousness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Alprazolam is recommended for short-term use in treating delirium.
items.reason: It is a short-acting benzodiazepine that many patients do not become dependent on long-term.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; alprazolam should be avoided as many patients become dependent on it long-term.

items.assertion: Incontinence and OAB are associated with social isolation and depression.
items.reason: They can be a precipitating factor in the decision to seek nursing home care when not manageable at home.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the association.

items.assertion: The history and physical examination should focus on identifying potentially reversible causes.
items.reason: This approach helps in managing urinary incontinence effectively.

items.assertion: Elder abuse and neglect can result in worsening of chronic medical conditions.
items.reason: Abuse and neglect lead to physical injuries, dehydration, pressure ulcers, emotional distress, and financial loss.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why elder abuse and neglect can worsen chronic medical conditions.

items.reason: The epidemic heightened awareness of necessary changes for long-term care quality.
